11: A direct revelation: 'For the life of the flesh is in the blood, and I have given it for you on the altar to make atonement for your souls, for it is the blood that makes atonement by the life.' This verse is the theological axiom that explains why Christ had to die a bloody death — not a natural death, not a symbolic death, but a sacrificial death involving the shedding of blood. The divine appointment ('I have given it for you on the altar') means that blood-atonement is not a primitive accommodation to human psychology but a divine mechanism built into creation-order: life given for life, blood for blood. Christ's death fulfills this principle at its ultimate level: his blood, the blood of the God-man, atones not for one year or one nation but once for all humanity (Hebrews 9:12).
1: YHWH spoke to Moses — the centralization law of Leviticus 17 is covenant-foundational: all slaughter of sacrificial animals must come to the tabernacle. The exclusivity principle encoded here points to Christ's exclusive claim: 'I am the way, and the truth, and the life. No one comes to the Father except through me' (John 14:6). Just as all valid sacrifice must go to the one sanctuary, all access to the Father goes through the one Mediator.
2: Speak to Aaron and his sons and to all the people — the double address (priestly and lay) of the blood legislation. Christ's sacrifice establishes the same universal standard: his blood is not a priestly prerogative but the universal atonement available to all: 'there is one God, and there is one mediator between God and men, the man Christ Jesus, who gave himself as a ransom for all' (1 Timothy 2:5-6).
3: If any one of the house of Israel kills an ox or a lamb or a goat in the camp or outside the camp — all slaughter of these species anywhere must be brought to the sanctuary. The prohibition of unauthorized sacrifice reflects the principle that human beings cannot generate their own access to God; the sacrifice must come through the divinely appointed place and priest. Christ is 'the one mediator' precisely because all human attempts to approach God outside of his mediation are, in the type's language, unauthorized field slaughter to goat-demons.
4: And does not bring it to the entrance of the tent of meeting — bloodguilt for unauthorized sacrifice incurred as though human blood were shed. The severity — treating animal sacrifice outside the sanctuary as equivalent to murder — reflects that unauthorized appropriation of the atoning mechanism is a capital offense against the sovereignty of the one who appointed it. All human-devised 'atonement' outside of Christ's appointed sacrifice incurs the same kind of illegitimacy.
5: That the people may bring their sacrifices to YHWH at the entrance of the tent of meeting — the positive purpose of the centralization law: all sacrifice becomes a genuine encounter with YHWH rather than unauthorized deal-making with other powers. The gathering of all worship to the one sanctuary is the type of all worship gathered to the one risen Lord: 'in him all things hold together' (Colossians 1:17); 'all authority in heaven and on earth has been given to me' (Matthew 28:18).
6: The priest shall throw the blood on the altar and burn the fat for a pleasing aroma to YHWH — the re-channeled field slaughter becomes legitimate peace offering with the standard blood-fat ritual. What was illegitimate outside becomes legitimate when brought to the appointed place and mediator. Christ's mediation transforms unauthorized human grasping into legitimate covenant approach: 'through him we both have access in one Spirit to the Father' (Ephesians 2:18).
7: No more sacrificing to the goat demons after whom they whore — the se'irim (goat-demons, wilderness spirits) represent all alternative spiritual authorities. Christ's cross disarms every competing spiritual power: 'he disarmed the rulers and authorities and put them to open shame, by triumphing over them in him' (Colossians 2:15). The prohibition on se'irim sacrifice is fulfilled in Christ's victory over the 'rulers and authorities' who are the NT counterpart to the se'irim of the wilderness.
8: Any one of the house of Israel or of the strangers who sojourns among them — the universal scope of the centralization law includes the resident Gentile. Christ's sacrifice is explicitly for all peoples: 'the Lamb who was slain' who 'ransomed people for God from every tribe and language and people and nation' (Revelation 5:9). The inclusion of the sojourner in the blood legislation is the type of the inclusion of the Gentiles in the new covenant atonement.
9: Who offers a burnt offering or sacrifice and does not bring it to the entrance of the tent — karet for unauthorized sacrifice by Israelite or sojourner. The exclusivity of the one sanctuary prepares for the exclusivity of the one sacrifice. Christ is 'the stone the builders rejected' who has become 'the cornerstone' and about whom Peter declares: 'there is salvation in no one else, for there is no other name under heaven given among men by which we must be saved' (Acts 4:11-12).
10: If any one eats any blood, I will set my face against that person and will cut him off — the divine first-person threat against blood consumption. Yet Christ commands precisely this: 'unless you eat the flesh of the Son of Man and drink his blood, you have no life in you' (John 6:53). The apparent reversal is resolved by recognizing that the blood prohibition was in force precisely because blood was reserved for the atonement — and when Christ gives his blood as the final atonement, the 'drinking' of his blood is the receiving of that atoning gift. The prohibition protects the type until the antitype arrives and transforms the prohibition into invitation.
12: No person among you shall eat blood, and the stranger who sojourns among you shall not eat blood — the universal blood prohibition for all in Israel. The prohibition's universality is the mirror of the universal invitation in Christ: 'Come, everyone who thirsts, come to the waters' (Isaiah 55:1). The universal prohibition that guarded the blood-as-nefesh for the atonement becomes the universal invitation when the blood-as-nefesh of Christ is offered for all.
13: Any one who takes a beast or bird that may be eaten shall pour out its blood and cover it with earth — the earth-covering of game blood. Christ's blood was not merely shed but 'covered' — buried: 'I delivered to you as of first importance... that Christ died for our sins in accordance with the Scriptures, that he was buried' (1 Corinthians 15:3-4). The burial is an integral part of the atonement sequence, not merely a disposal; the earth that covered the game's blood is the type of the tomb that 'covered' the blood of the Lamb until the resurrection reversed the covering.
14: For the life of every creature is its blood: its blood is its life — the nefesh-blood identification restated for all creatures. The life of Christ — his divine-human nefesh — is uniquely present in his blood: 'This cup that is poured out for you is the new covenant in my blood' (Luke 22:20). The life of the Son of God poured out in death is the ultimate fulfillment of the nefesh-in-blood principle: the blood that atones does so precisely because it carries the infinite life of the eternal Son.
15: Whoever eats what died of itself or what was torn by beasts shall be unclean until evening — neveilah and terefah (animals that died without proper blood-draining) create impurity because the blood remained in them. The proper draining of blood at slaughter is the type of the proper 'pouring out' of Christ's blood in the sacrifice: 'he poured out his soul to death' (Isaiah 53:12, heereh lammavet nafsho). Christ is neither neveilah (died of itself) nor terefah (torn randomly) but the appointed sacrificial lamb whose blood was properly given at the appointed time.
16: If he does not wash them or bathe, he shall bear his iniquity — the purification requirement after eating improperly-bled meat. The principle that proximity to death (even animal death) requires purification points to the radical purification that proximity to Christ's death accomplishes: 'Do you not know that all of us who have been baptized into Christ Jesus were baptized into his death? We were buried therefore with him by baptism into death, in order that, just as Christ was raised from the dead by the glory of the Father, we too might walk in newness of life' (Romans 6:3-4). The laundering-and-bathing of the type becomes the baptismal dying-and-rising of the antitype.
